1:
With the temple at last finished, the witnesses gather first around the quiet act that opens the chapter: Solomon bringing in the things his father David had dedicated. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the verse is all but identical with 1 Kings 7:51, the Chronicler making only three slight corrections of the older text; what the Authorised Version calls the dedicated things and implements he renders more exactly as the hallowed vessels — the silver and the gold — belonging to David, and he points to the earlier record of that treasure in 1 Chronicles 18:11 and 1 Chron. 26:26-28. With this verse, he observes, the great section on the dedication of the temple begins, and the whole is an almost literal duplicate of the parallel account. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds only that the gold was the remainder of those vast sums David had amassed, recorded at 1 Chron. 22:14.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the bare inventory into a lesson in fidelity. There needed no great solemnity for bringing in the dedicated things; they added to the wealth and beauty of the house but could not add to its holiness, for it was the temple that sanctified the gold, as our Lord would later argue in Matt. 23:17. Henry marvels at Solomon's justice both to God and to his father: whatever David had devoted, however much the son might have coveted it, he would by no means alienate but laid among the temple treasures. Children who would inherit their godly parents' blessing, he presses, must religiously pursue their pious intentions and not defeat them; and when Solomon had made all the vessels in abundance, as 2 Chron. 4:18 had told, the materials left over he reserved for a time of need rather than turn to common use — to divert what is once dedicated to God being nothing less than sacrilege.

Yet the ark itself, Henry notes, called for great solemnity, and received it. The other vessels were made new and larger, but the ark, with its mercy-seat and cherubim, was the very same that had stood in the tabernacle; for the presence and grace of God are the same in a little assembly as in a great one, and wherever two or three are gathered in Christ's name he is as truly present as among thousands, the promise of Matt. 18:20. The ark, he says, was a type of Christ and a token of God's presence, so that the pledge "Lo, I am with you always" in Matt. 28:20 in effect brings the ark into every worshipping assembly that pleads it by faith and prayer, while a temple Christ has left is desolate, as in Matt. 23:38. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, surveying the whole chapter, traces its movement: Solomon assembles the elders and Levites, fetches the ark up from the city of David, offers sacrifices past numbering, and sets it beneath the cherubim's wings, until the singers and trumpeters praise God and his glory fills the house so that the priests can no longer stand to minister. The voices do not divide; together they frame the moment when the finished house receives both the treasures of the father and, far above them, the presence of God.
2:
The transfer of the ark is the first great act of the dedication, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses attend closely to its assembly, its starting-point, and its date. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark this as the opening movement of the whole celebration — the carrying up of the ark from Mount Zion to the temple, to which Solomon's blessing in the following chapter belongs — and note that the transaction has already been expounded at the parallel in 1 Kings 8:1-21, so that only the divergences need remark here. The chief of these is chronological: the Chronicler omits the name of the month, Ethanim, which 1 Kings 8:2 supplies, leaving only the bare clause "that is, the seventh month," so that the month's name must be either understood or restored. Whatever the state of the text, they insist, the feast was held in the seventh month.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens both the persons and the wording. "The chief of the fathers" he renders more precisely as the chiefs of the clans, the heads of the father-houses — the Vulgate's "heads of families" — the elders and heads being, in the idiom of the East, the sheikhs and emirs of the tribes. He agrees that the words "in the month of Ethanim" have dropped accidentally out of the Hebrew before the phrase "in the feast," the Authorised Version's punctuation suggesting one way of mending the gap and the Syriac another; he compares the keeping of the feast of the seventh month in Nehemiah 8:14; and he observes that where the Hebrew fixes the ark's starting-point as the city of David, "which is Zion," the Syriac and Arabic read "Hebron" instead, though the Hebrew text and the older narrative alike keep Zion.

Beneath the textual fog the event stands clear, and the witnesses let it stand. At the great autumn feast Solomon summoned to Jerusalem the elders of Israel and every tribal and clan head, that the ark of the covenant might be carried up from the city of David to the house now made ready for it. The choice of Zion as the starting-point is no accident of geography: it was there, in the tent David had pitched, that the ark had rested since he first brought it up, and from that older sanctuary it is now borne the short way to its permanent home. The Chronicler, the witnesses observe, is content to abridge and occasionally to blur the account he copies; but the date and the place, once the copyists' slips are mended, are firm — at the feast of the seventh month the whole leadership of the nation gathered as one to see the ark of the LORD brought home to its resting-place.
3:
"All the men of Israel assembled themselves unto the king in the feast which was in the seventh month" — and here the witnesses take up the question that will run through the next verses, who bore the ark, the priests or the Levites, together with the meaning of the chosen season. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that where the Chronicler writes that the Levites took up the ark, the parallel account has the priests bear it; and since even the Chronicler himself, at v.7, has the priests carry the ark into the holy place, the "Levites" here must be understood of such Levites as were also priests. A little further on, they add, the awkward phrase "the priests the Levites" is to be corrected from 1 Kings 8:4 to "the priests and the Levites," the connecting word having slipped from the text under a copyist's eye; for while the priests bore the ark and its holy vessels, the dismantled tabernacle itself — its planks, hangings, and coverings — was carried by the ordinary Levites, to be preserved in the temple as a venerable relic. They note as well a small copying-slip nearby, a letter dropped or doubled, the kind of minor blemish that dogs any text handed down by scribes.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the occasion. "The feast" is the feast of Tabernacles, kept in the seventh month, as the Targum explains and as the older narrative at 1 Kings 8:2 confirms — the autumn festival of ingathering, when the harvest was home and all Israel came up to Jerusalem.

The date, once named, carries its own fitness. Tabernacles was the feast at which Israel dwelt in booths, remembering the wilderness years when the LORD had tabernacled in the midst of his people and led them by the cloud; and it is at that feast, above all others, that the ark — the very token of God's dwelling with Israel — is brought into the house built to enshrine it, there to be answered, before the chapter closes, by the cloud of glory. The witnesses do not press the typology, but the reverent order they expound serves it: the elders and all the men of Israel converging on the king, the sacred procession moving off in its graded ranks, the priests set apart to the holiest burden and the Levites to the rest. That the priests alone touched the ark while the Levites bore the tent and its furniture was no arbitrary rule but a picture of graded holiness — the nearer a thing stood to the presence of God, the more consecrated the hands that might carry it.
4:
A single grammatical-historical voice attends this verse, and the point he presses is the same textual crux the narrative keeps circling — whether it was the Levites or the priests who came and took up the ark. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that where the Chronicler writes "the Levites," the older account has "the priests," and the Syriac and Arabic follow it even here, while the Septuagint and the Vulgate keep "Levites." The divergence, he explains, is more apparent than real: the tribal name Levi may of course be used broadly, to embrace the priests, the sons of Aaron, as well as their inferior brethren of the same tribe; and the next verses settle the sense, for v.7 shows plainly that it was the priests who were intended, since it is they who bear the ark into the most holy place.

That the Greek and Latin translators preferred the wider term, while the Syriac and Arabic conformed it to the reading of the parallel, only shows how early and how carefully the two records were compared. The observation is a small model of the grammatical-historical method — Scripture interpreted by Scripture, the ambiguous term resolved not by conjecture but by the writer's own usage a few lines on. The Chronicler, whose interest in the Levitical orders runs through his whole book, is content to name the wider tribe where the older account names the narrower office, confident that no reader would suppose the ark handled by any but its consecrated bearers.

So the seeming clash between the two histories dissolves once the breadth of the name is felt: it was Levites who came, and among them the priests, the sons of Aaron, who alone might lift the ark of the covenant onto their shoulders and carry it up to the house prepared for it. Ellicott leaves the verse as an instance of how the versions may differ over a word while the history they report stands undisturbed — a reminder that the reverence of the transfer, and not the label of its bearers, is the thing the text would have us see.
5:
With the ark now lifted, the witnesses follow the whole sacred convoy up to the temple — the ark, the tabernacle of the congregation, and all the holy vessels that were in it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the procession and its careful division of labour: the priests, who were Levites, bore the ark and the sacred utensils, but the tabernacle itself — its planks, hangings, and coverings — was carried by the Levites, to be laid up in the temple as a venerable relic rather than melted down or put to new use. The awkward Hebrew "the priests the Levites," they add, is to be corrected from 1 Kings 8:4 to "the priests and the Levites," the copula having dropped out under a scribe's hand.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees on the reading. The phrase some would explain as "the Levitical priests," after the manner of Joshua 3:3; but the weight of the Hebrew manuscripts, the Septuagint, the Syriac, and above all the parallel at 1 Kings 8:4 favours "the priests and the Levites," so that the Authorised Version rightly supplies the conjunction that the Chronicler's text had lost.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lifts the eye from the grammar to the meaning. The ark, he says, was a type of Christ and a token of the presence of God, so that the promise "Lo, I am with you always, even unto the end of the world" in effect brings the ark into our worshipping assemblies when we claim it by faith and prayer — and a temple Christ has forsaken is a desolate place. As for the old tabernacle-vessels now superseded by the larger furniture of the temple, Wesley marks the reverent economy: as many as were fit for use were probably still used, and the rest carefully laid up as monuments of antiquity. The witnesses do not divide; between the grammarians' care for the text and Wesley's care for its sense, the verse shows a whole apparatus of the older worship gathered up into the new house, nothing once dedicated to God discarded, and the ark itself borne to its place as the abiding sign of his presence with his people.
6:
The Chronicler pauses on the sacrifices, and the lone grammatical-historical witness weighs the very words. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the verb rendered "assembled" is not the one used in the previous verses: there the word meant simply to call the people together, but here it is the term for meeting at an appointed time and place — the same root that describes God's own promise to "meet" with Israel at the ark in Exodus 25:22, "there I will meet with thee, and I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat." The congregation, in other words, is not merely summoned to a ceremony but keeps the appointed tryst God had fixed at the ark from the beginning. The distinction is not the pedant's, for the two roots look back to different institutions: the one gathered the nation for counsel or war, the other named the sacred rendezvous of the tent of meeting itself, so that the word chosen here quietly casts the whole scene as an act of appointed, covenant worship.

By the loss of a single Hebrew word, Ellicott notes, the whole form of the sentence has been altered from that of the older account, where the people are said to be "with him before the ark, sacrificing." The verb itself is best rendered as a continuous "were sacrificing," an act drawn out and prolonged rather than a single offering; and the victims were sheep and oxen "which could not be told nor numbered for multitude," a lavishness the parallel had marked in the very same terms. The change of construction is small but not idle: it binds king and congregation together in the one act, so that it is not Solomon sacrificing while the people look on, but the whole assembly gathered with him before the ark, their offerings ascending together.

What at first glance is merely a note of extravagant sacrifice becomes, under the grammarian's eye, a picture of the propriety of the day. Israel does not stumble upon her God; she comes to the very place and hour of his own choosing, the ark over which he had pledged to commune with her, and there she pours out worship without measure. The uncountable multitude of victims is not waste but proportion — an offering large enough, if any could be, for the once-for-all bringing of the ark into its rest. And the continuous verb keeps the scene before the eye as a thing still going on: the beasts slain in unbroken succession, the smoke rising without pause, the whole nation kept at its appointed meeting-place before the LORD, until the priests should carry the ark the last few steps into the darkness of the most holy place.
8:
The cherubim over the ark draw the single grammatical-historical witness to a fine point of Hebrew, and behind it to the whole image of the shrine. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott would render the opening "And the cherubim were spreading forth their wings" rather than the Authorised Version's "For the cherubims spread forth their wings"; the older account, he observes, has the little connecting word "for," which makes the clause an explanation of the last words of v.7 — the ark was set beneath the cherubim precisely because their outspread wings overshadowed its place. Drop that one word, and a reason becomes a mere description, the spreading of the wings reported as a fact standing beside the ark's placing rather than accounting for it.

He notes a second, finer difference. Where the Chronicler writes that the cherubim "covered" the ark and its staves, the parallel uses the technical term for the cherubim overshadowing the ark, the very word employed of the golden cherubim upon the mercy-seat in Exodus 25:20, and of the pattern David gave for these greater ones in 1 Chronicles 28:18. In Hebrew the two readings differ only by the transposition of a single letter, so that the Chronicler's word may well be a clerical slip for the older and more exact expression, though the sense is untouched. That the older text should use, for these colossal figures, the very verb Moses used of the little golden cherubim over the mercy-seat is itself telling: the Chronicler's temple does not break with the tabernacle but fulfils it on a greater scale, the same overshadowing repeated now in vast wings of cedar overlaid with gold.

The cross-references Ellicott gathers open the picture the verse means to fix in the mind, for two sets of cherubim meet in the darkness of the shrine. The ark itself carried the small golden pair beaten out of the mercy-seat, their wings stretched over the place of atonement as in Exodus 25:20; and over these Solomon had set two far greater figures, made after the pattern David delivered, their wings spanning the whole inner room. So the ark comes to rest beneath a canopy above a canopy — the older wings of the mercy-seat sheltering the covenant tables, and the vast wings of the guardian cherubim arching over the whole sacred chest and even the poles by which it had been borne. Whether the text says the wings "spread" or "covered," whether the clause explains or merely describes, the image is one: the throne of the invisible God overshadowed in the inmost dark, prepared for the glory the closing verses will bring down upon it.
9:
The drawing out of the staves is the chapter's small mystery, and the witnesses divide over what its closing words preserve. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the plain sense: the staves were drawn out because, the ark being now settled in its final place and no longer to be carried about, they had become unnecessary. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verse more exactly — "the staves were so long that the tips of the staves were seen" — and explains the odd geometry: the priests standing in the great hall could see the ends of the poles projecting from within the most holy place, drawn just far enough forward to be visible before the oracle, while those outside the hall could not see them at all. Where the Chronicler writes "seen from the ark," the older account and the Septuagint read "from the holy place," which Ellicott takes to be original.

Then the last clause opens a genuine divergence. "And there it is unto this day" — but is it the ark that remained, or the staves? Ellicott lays the readings side by side: the Vulgate makes it the ark ("so the ark was there"), and the received Hebrew can be so construed; but the Septuagint, Syriac, Targum, and Arabic, with several Hebrew manuscripts and the parallel at 1 Kings 8:8, make it the staves that "were there," a reading he judges no doubt right, a single letter having fallen from the text.

And here Ellicott draws his most striking observation, one the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley shares. The note "unto this day" cannot speak from the Chronicler's own age, for by the time he wrote — after the return from Babylon — neither ark nor staves remained to be seen; Wesley fixes the phrase to the day the history was first written, not to Ezra's later review of it. Ellicott presses the point into a general law of ancient authorship: that the Chronicler copied the remark from his source without altering it to suit the changed circumstances, exactly as the compiler of the older book had done long before him, is a striking instance of how Oriental historians borrow with literal exactitude from their predecessors, even where such fidelity has grown, to a modern eye, plainly infelicitous. The three witnesses thus leave the verse doubly instructive: a vanished set of staves whose projecting tips once marked the hidden ark, and a fossil phrase whose "unto this day" quietly betrays the older document beneath the book we now read.
10:
"There was nothing in the ark save the two tables" — and the witnesses dwell on those tables, on the wording that records them, and on the covenant they were. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the fuller expansion of the Chaldee paraphrase, which specifies that nothing lay in the ark but the two tables Moses placed there after the first pair had been broken on account of the calf the people made at Horeb — the second tables being those afterward confirmed, written with the writing of the Ten Words. Clarke's interest is in that history of breaking and renewal: the ark's treasure is not the first covenant shattered in Israel's sin but the second, graciously given again, so that the very stones enshrined at the heart of the temple are a monument of mercy after apostasy.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the exactness of the record. The Chronicler writes that Moses "put" the tables in the ark, where the older account, fuller and clearer, says he "placed" them there; the change, Ellicott suggests, may echo the language of Exodus 40:20, "and he put the testimony into the ark," while the small word "there" has fallen out of the Chronicler's text. The closing phrase "when the LORD made a covenant with the children of Israel" he reads as pointing to the tables themselves as the covenant — the two tablets which the LORD covenanted with his people — comparing the ark described as that "wherein is the covenant of the LORD" at 2 Chron. 6:11 and its parallel at 1 Kings 8:21.

The emptiness of the ark is eloquent, and the witnesses feel it. The most sacred object in Israel, enthroned in the darkness beneath the cherubim, contained neither the golden pot of manna nor Aaron's rod that budded, laid up before the testimony in the wilderness, but only the two tables of stone — nothing save the terms of the covenant God had made at Horeb, broken once and mercifully renewed. That the ark held the law and nothing else declares what the whole costly house was built to shelter: not a hoard of treasure, for all the gold and silver just brought in enriched the temple but was not its reason for being; the reason lay here, in two plain tablets of stone, because on them was written the bond between the LORD and his people, set at the very centre of the nation's worship, beneath the wings of the cherubim and about to be crowned with his glory.
11:
As the priests come out of the holy place, the Chronicler opens the long passage that is peculiarly his own — the ministry of the Levitical singers — and the witnesses gather around both the detail and its glory. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that this and the two following verses describe the part the priests and Levitical musicians took in the transfer of the ark, a matter the parallel narrative passes over, confining itself to the main transaction. The mention of the priests occasions the remark that all who were present had sanctified themselves, but the courses were not to be observed — the twenty-four divisions appointed in 1 Chronicles 24 could not be kept, for the festival was so great that not merely the course on duty that week but every course had consecrated itself and shared in the service. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms that from this point the narrative is the Chronicler's own, inserted as a long parenthesis into the two halves of the single verse at 1 Kings 8:10; "that were present" he renders "that could be found," as at 2 Kings 19:4, and "were sanctified" as "had purified themselves" for the ceremony, after the manner of 1 Chron. 15:12 — not the one class then on duty, but all the classes without distinction. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes the same point: the courses of David's appointment in 1 Chron. 25 were for the ordinary service, but at extraordinary solemnities such as this they all came together; and Adam Clarke adds only that the priests came out after carrying the ark into the holy of holies, before the sacred service of praise began.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers all this into the chapter's central wonder — God taking possession of his house. That the priests must all come out, he says, is the very way of giving possession: all must withdraw that the rightful owner may enter, and would we have God dwell in our hearts, we must leave room for him and let everything else give way. He notes that the whole family of priests attended, not one course only, every man sanctified, because there was work enough for them all and because it was fit they should be eye-witnesses of the solemnity and receive its impressions. And Henry begins here the reflection he will carry through the cloud: that God filled the house not when the sacrifices were offered but when the singers praised him, for the sacrifice of praise pleases the LORD better than an ox, as Ps. 69:31 has it. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the emptying of the holy place of its ministers is no interruption but a preparation — the servants stepping back so that the Master may fill the house, as the very next verses will tell.
12:
Now the Chronicler arrays the choir, and the witnesses catalogue it with him. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the roll: the Levite singers, all of them — Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun, with their sons and their brethren — clad in white byssus, with cymbals, psalteries, and harps, standing at the east end of the altar, and with them a hundred and twenty priests sounding trumpets. All the Levitic singers and players, they note, were engaged to make the festival glorious with song and instrument, and they point back to the fuller account of these guilds, their vesture, and their instruments in 1 Chronicles 15:17-28 and 1 Chron. 15:24. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the Chronicler's very syntax — the repeated particle "to" before each name, "to Asaph, to Heman," and "to their sons, to their brethren" — a construction so characteristic of this writer that the style of these verses stands in marked contrast with the older, borrowed narrative of the chapter's first half. He identifies the instruments as cymbals, lutes, and lyres, the white linen dress from 1 Chron. 15:27, and reckons the hundred and twenty trumpeting priests as five to each of the twenty-four courses; the parenthesis-marks of the Authorised Version, he adds, should be cancelled, for this is no aside but the heart of the scene.

Against this fullness the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enters a blunt dissent about the sound itself. Cymbals, psalteries, and harps joined to a hundred and twenty trumpets or horns, he protests, could have produced little harmony, and as for melody it was simply impossible, the noise being too great. The others do not answer him, for their interest lies in the ordered array rather than its acoustics; but the difference is real. Where Clarke hears a deafening din, Keil and Ellicott see a deliberately marshalled magnificence — every guild and instrument and priestly course drawn up in its place, the whole worship of Israel gathered into one great company that stood ready, east of the altar, for the single act of praise to which the next verse rises.
13:
The whole array now becomes a single voice, and at that instant the cloud descends. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch labour to bring out the unison the Hebrew presses so hard: it came to pass, "as one," that the trumpeters and the singers sang with one voice to praise and thank Jahve — the phrase "as one" thrown to the front for emphasis, the subject stressed, the whole grammar bent to say that the many players and singers made, quite literally, one sound. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it to the same effect — "the trumpeters and the minstrels were to sound aloud, as one man, with one sound, to praise and thank the LORD" — and marks that with these words the long Levitical parenthesis ends and the borrowed narrative resumes. The song itself, he notes, is the common liturgical refrain of Israel's worship, "For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever," the very formula of 1 Chronicles 16:34 and 1 Chron. 16:41.

Then the cloud. Ellicott would strike the Authorised Version's "that," reading with the older account at 1 Kings 8:10 simply "then the cloud filled the house of the LORD." He weighs the versions — the Septuagint's fuller "the cloud of the glory of the LORD," the plainer Vulgate — and defends the received text against a possible rendering "the cloud of the house of the LORD," taking the repeated phrase "the house of the LORD" as a deliberate climax, so that the emphatic word at the last is Jehovah himself, of whose presence the bright cloud was the standing token.

Both witnesses hold the same two things together: the moment of the wonder is exactly the moment of united praise, and the cloud is no vague vapour but the visible sign of the LORD's own coming to dwell in the house prepared for him. The trumpets and the voices become one; they sing of a mercy that endures for ever; and heaven answers by filling the temple — the divine response timed not to the blood of sacrifice but to the song of thanksgiving, as if to teach that what draws God down among his people is the united, wholehearted praise of a mercy that never ends.
14:
The chapter ends in the overwhelming — "the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud, for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply note that this verse agrees with the parallel at 1 Kings 8:11, where it has already been expounded. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording: where the older account says "the house of the LORD," the Chronicler writes "the house of God," one of his habitual retouchings and of no doctrinal weight; and "to stand to minister" he glosses as to take their places, to draw near to serve, comparing Solomon's own standing before the altar in 2 Chron. 6:12 and the like scene at 2 Chron. 7:2, where the same glory again bars the priests from entering the house.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke abandons the grammar for wonder: "What a proof of the being of God, and of the Divine presence! What must those holy men have felt at this time!" The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the cloud as God's own act of taking possession, and finds in it his acceptance of the house: as he had made his public entry into the tabernacle of Moses by a cloud in Exodus 40:34, so now he owns the temple to be the same to him that the tabernacle was. The cloud, Henry adds, was spread as a veil, in mercy to a people who could not have borne the naked lustre of the divine light, as God is said to spread his cloud upon the face of his throne in Job 26:9.

And Henry hears in the priests' forced withdrawal a note that reaches beyond the day. That the ministers of the law could not stand to serve was a plain intimation, he says, that the Levitical priesthood must one day cease and stand to minister no longer, when the Messiah should come, in whom the fulness of the Godhead should dwell bodily; in him the glory of God would dwell among men, though covered with a cloud, for the Word was made flesh, and when he comes to his temple, like a refiner's fire, who may abide the day of his coming, as Malachi 3:1 asks. So the witnesses agree in reading the moment not as judgment but as acceptance: the glory that drove the priests from the sanctuary is the same glory that had crowned the tabernacle of old, the visible seal that God had taken the temple for his own — the house finished, the ark carried home, the covenant resting in its heart, the singers lifted up as one, and now the LORD himself filling the place he had chosen to dwell in among his people.
